questions.question: What is HTTP/2 connection coalescing?
options: Opening multiple TLS connections to the same server for parallel requests
Reusing one existing h2 connection for requests to additional origins that share the same IP and a valid TLS certificate
Merging multiple HTTP/1.1 keep-alive connections into one TCP session
Compressing HPACK header tables across different browser tabs
questions.explanation: Connection coalescing (RFC 9113 §9.1) lets a client reuse an existing HTTP/2 connection for a new origin when all three conditions hold: same resolved IP, the existing TLS certificate covers the new hostname (SAN match), and ALPN negotiated h2. No second handshake is required.

questions.question: What is the default value of the HTTP/2 connection-level flow-control window, and which frame replenishes it?
options: 16384 bytes, replenished by PUSH_PROMISE
65535 bytes, replenished by WINDOW_UPDATE targeting stream ID 0
131072 bytes, replenished by RST_STREAM
65535 bytes, replenished by SETTINGS_INITIAL_WINDOW_SIZE on every new stream
questions.explanation: RFC 9113 sets the default initial connection window to 65535 bytes. A sender consuming that budget must wait for the receiver to issue a WINDOW_UPDATE frame with stream ID 0 (connection-level) before it can send more DATA frames on any stream.

questions.question: In the 8-step failure scenario, why do streams 3 and 5 stall even though their per-stream windows are still 65535 bytes?
options: The server has closed streams 3 and 5 with RST_STREAM
SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS was set to 1 by the server
The connection-level window reached zero after stream 1 sent 65535 bytes, and the client never issued a WINDOW_UPDATE
The TLS certificate does not list b.example and c.example in its SAN field
questions.explanation: HTTP/2 senders must respect bytes_allowed = min(conn_window, stream_window). When stream 1 exhausted the 65535-byte connection window, conn_window reached 0. Even with healthy per-stream windows, streams 3 and 5 cannot send any DATA until the connection window is replenished by a WINDOW_UPDATE(stream=0).

questions.question: Which combination of SETTINGS parameters does a malicious middlebox most commonly rewrite to cause coalescing-induced stalls?
options: SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE and SETTINGS_ENABLE_PUSH
SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS lowered to 1 and SETTINGS_INITIAL_WINDOW_SIZE lowered to 4096
SETTINGS_MAX_FRAME_SIZE raised above 16384 and SETTINGS_ENABLE_CONNECT_PROTOCOL enabled
SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE lowered to 0 and SETTINGS_ENABLE_PUSH disabled
questions.explanation: A TLS-terminating proxy may lower MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS to 1, serialising all coalesced streams so each must wait for the previous to finish, or lower INITIAL_WINDOW_SIZE to 4096, causing every stream to exhaust its window after just one small DATA frame. Both are diagnostic signatures visible in nghttp -v or Wireshark compared against origin server config.

questions.question: What exactly does the WINDOW_UPDATE frame sent in step 6 of the Build-It procedure look like in hex, and why is stream ID 0 used?
options: 00 00 04 08 00 00 00 00 01 | 00 00 ff ff; stream ID 1 targets only stream 1
00 00 04 08 00 00 00 00 00 | 00 00 ff ff; stream ID 0 targets the connection-level window
00 00 00 04 01 00 00 00 00; this is a SETTINGS ACK, not a WINDOW_UPDATE
00 00 0c 04 00 00 00 00 00 | 00 03 00 00 00 64; this is a new SETTINGS frame
questions.explanation: The frame header encodes length=4 (00 00 04), type=0x8 (WINDOW_UPDATE), flags=0x0, stream_id=0 (00 00 00 00), followed by the 31-bit increment 65535 (00 00 ff ff). Stream ID 0 means the increment applies to the shared connection window, allowing all blocked streams to resume sending DATA.

questions.question: How should an operator permanently prevent the coalescing-induced deadlock from recurring in production?
options: Increase SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS to 1000 on the server
Move the coalesced origins to distinct IP addresses so the same-IP coalescing precondition fails, or issue separate TLS certificates that do not share SAN entries
Disable HPACK compression so header overhead cannot fill the connection window
Switch to HTTP/1.1 keep-alive for all three origins to avoid stream multiplexing entirely
questions.explanation: The most durable mitigations target the coalescing preconditions themselves: assigning distinct IPs to different origins (breaks condition 1) or issuing separate TLS certificates with non-overlapping SAN lists (breaks condition 2). This ensures the browser opens independent connections and a stall on one cannot cascade to the others. Tuning WINDOW_UPDATE cadence helps but does not eliminate the risk if the same coalescing topology remains.
